title: Light the Candle When You Walk In
subtitle: A daily home ritual for marking the transition from outside to inside
excerpt: A small daily ritual for the return home. Light a candle when you walk in, every day. Not when the house is clean. Not when the week has gone well. Every day. The practice marks the threshold and changes the quality of what follows.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Candle-lighting as a threshold ritual appears across many traditions and cultures as a way of marking arrival and creating a distinct quality of attention.

content.text: A small daily ritual for the moment of return. It takes thirty seconds. The effect on the quality of the evening is disproportionate to the time.
Keep a candle and matches or a lighter in the same spot near the front door, or wherever you first put things down. The ritual only works if the candle is accessible without searching for it.
I am ready to release the outside world and everything I carried it in. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
I am ready to allow this home to be a different kind of place from what I just left. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.
Say each quietly while you light the candle. Or say nothing. The action is the practice.
Watch the flame for ten seconds. Not as a spiritual act if that doesn't fit. Simply as a pause between what was and what is now. The pause is what the candle creates.
Say it or think it. Then go about the evening. Blow the candle out when you go to bed.

Written for homemade.education, drawing on the long tradition of candle-lighting as a threshold marker. Threshold rituals appear in domestic traditions worldwide as a way of acknowledging the change from one context to another. The release-and-allow structure draws on the framework from The Money Zone (Rebecca Page, 2024).
